Name: task_specific_bit_allocation
Title: Task-Specific Bit Allocation for Model Compression: An Information-Theoretic Approach
Short Hypothesis: The optimal bit allocation for model compression varies significantly across tasks, and explicitly quantifying task-specific information needs can lead to more efficient compression without sacrificing performance.
Related Work: Existing work in model compression, such as pruning and quantization, often treats all tasks uniformly, ignoring task-specific information requirements. Recent information-theoretic approaches (e.g., Isik et al., Bu et al.) focus on rate-distortion tradeoffs but do not explicitly account for task-specific information. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing task-specific bit allocation, grounded in mutual information and task-relevant distortion metrics, which has not been explored in the literature.
Abstract: Model compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, often treat all tasks uniformly, ignoring the inherent differences in their information requirements. We propose a novel framework for task-specific bit allocation, grounded in information theory, that explicitly quantifies the information needs of different tasks and allocates bits accordingly. By leveraging mutual information and task-relevant distortion metrics, our approach identifies which components of a model are most critical for specific tasks and allocates bits to preserve task-specific information. We derive theoretical bounds on the bit allocation tradeoffs and validate our approach on a diverse set of tasks, including image classification, language modeling, and reinforcement learning. Our experiments demonstrate that task-specific bit allocation achieves superior compression rates while maintaining or even improving task performance compared to uniform compression methods. This work bridges the gap between information theory and practical model compression, offering a principled and efficient approach to task-aware compression.
Experiments: Task-specific mutual information estimation: Compute mutual information between model components (e.g., layers, neurons) and task outputs for a diverse set of tasks (CIFAR-10, ImageNet, GPT-2 fine-tuning).

Bit allocation optimization: Formulate bit allocation as a constrained optimization problem where the objective is to minimize task-relevant distortion under a bit budget. Use gradient-based methods to solve for optimal bit allocation.

Compression evaluation: Compress models using task-specific bit allocation and compare against uniform compression baselines (e.g., pruning, quantization). Evaluate performance on task-specific metrics (accuracy, perplexity, reward) and compression efficiency (bits-per-weight).

Ablation studies: Investigate the impact of different distortion metrics (e.g., cross-entropy, mean squared error) and mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE, kernel-based estimators) on compression performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Mutual information estimation may be computationally expensive for large models and tasks.
The effectiveness of task-specific bit allocation depends on the availability of task-specific data, which may limit applicability in transfer learning scenarios.
The proposed method may not generalize well to tasks with highly overlapping information requirements.
